locked_facts: Both masses have SAME magnitude of acceleration
Heavier mass accelerates DOWN, lighter mass accelerates UP
If m₁ = m₂: system is in equilibrium, T = mg
Tension is always less than weight of heavier mass and more than weight of lighter mass
truth_statements: In an Atwood machine, acceleration depends on the difference and sum of masses
The tension in the string is the same throughout for a massless inextensible string
Both objects have the same magnitude of acceleration
Tension is less than the weight of the heavier mass
When both masses are equal, the system is in equilibrium with zero acceleration

aha_moment.what_to_show: Both masses shown with weight arrows (m₁g and m₂g) and tension arrow T. Labels clearly show T < m₁g on the heavy side and T > m₂g on the light side — simultaneously visible
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Numerical comparison: T, m₁g, and m₂g all labelled with values showing the inequality m₂g < T < m₁g
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see the tension is NOT equal to either weight — it is strictly between the two weight values

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Two masses hanging from a frictionless pulley via a massless inextensible string. Both at rest. Weight arrows shown for each mass. m₁ > m₂ labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: System at rest with the heavier mass on one side — about to be released

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: System released. Heavier mass descends, lighter mass rises. Acceleration arrows appear on both masses — same magnitude, opposite directions. Tension T shown in string.
simulation_states.emphasis: Both masses accelerate with the same magnitude — the string constraint forces equal acceleration

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Freeze frame with numerical values. On heavy mass: T arrow up, m₁g arrow down, with T < m₁g clearly labelled. On light mass: T arrow up, m₂g arrow down, with T > m₂g clearly labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: Tension is NOT equal to the weight of either mass. m₂g < T < m₁g. The inequality is the key insight.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust m₁ and m₂. See acceleration and tension update. When m₁ = m₂, acceleration = 0 and T = mg (equilibrium). When m₂ → 0, acceleration → g and T → 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: Try equal masses: system is in equilibrium. Make one much heavier: acceleration approaches g. Tension is always between the two weights.

common_misconceptions.belief: Tension in the string equals the weight of the heavier mass
trigger_phrases: tension equals heavy mass ka weight
string mein tension = bhari wala ka weight
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says for an Atwood machine T = 2m₁m₂g/(m₁+m₂), which is always LESS than m₁g. If T equalled m₁g, the heavier mass would not accelerate.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show T < m₁g and T > m₂g simultaneously with numerical values on both sides of the pulley

common_traps: assuming tension = weight of heavier mass
forgetting both masses have same acceleration
wrong sign in net force equation
